name: Maratha Talwar Sword
significance: This curved single-edged talwar is representative of the swords carried by Maratha light cavalry during Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj's campaigns. The brass disc hilt and slight blade curve allowed for swift mounted slashing strikes, a hallmark of Maratha guerrilla warfare tactics (Ganimi Kava).

name: Maratha Bichwa Dagger
significance: The bichwa, or 'scorpion-tail' dagger, was carried concealed by Maratha soldiers and court attendants. Its looped handle protected the knuckles while the double-curved blade made it effective in close combat, a design unique to the Deccan region.

name: Maratha Chainmail Armour (Zirah Baktar)
significance: Worn beneath a quilted tunic by cavalry officers, this mail shirt offered flexible protection against sword cuts while preserving mobility, essential for the fast hill-and-valley warfare practiced around the Sahyadri forts.

name: Shivrai Silver Coin
significance: Minted shortly after Shivaji Maharaj's coronation as Chhatrapati in 1674, the Shivrai coin bore Devanagari script and symbolised the establishment of independent Maratha sovereignty and an indigenous currency system, free of Mughal mint authority.

name: Peshwa-era Gold Mohur
significance: Struck during the administration of the Peshwas from Pune, this gold mohur reflects the economic prosperity and expanding trade networks of the Maratha Confederacy in the 18th century.

name: Satavahana Copper Coin
significance: Issued under the Satavahana dynasty, one of the earliest ruling powers of the Deccan plateau, this coin carries faint ship and Ujjain-symbol motifs that point to Maharashtra's ancient maritime and trade links.

name: Raigad Fort Scale Model
significance: A 1:500 scale architectural model depicting Raigad's Hirakani Buruj, Takmak Tok cliff, and the Maha Darwaza gateway, used by the museum's education wing to explain Maratha hill-fort defence engineering.

name: Sindhudurg Fort Model
significance: This model recreates the sea-fort built by Shivaji Maharaj on Kurte Island to safeguard the Konkan coastline and the Maratha navy, highlighting its distinctive curtain walls that follow the natural rock shoreline.

name: Lohagad Fort Model
significance: Lohagad's distinctive Vinchu Kata (scorpion's tail) fortification spur is reproduced in detail in this model, illustrating an unusual defensive design intended to cut off an attacking force's approach.

name: Basalt Temple Sculpture Fragment
significance: Carved under Rashtrakuta patronage in the volcanic basalt typical of the Deccan trap, this fragment depicts a dvarapala (gatekeeper deity) and reflects the rock-cut sculptural tradition that produced the nearby Kailasa Temple.

name: Bronze Vithoba Idol
significance: A devotional bronze of Vithoba, the presiding deity of the Pandharpur Wari pilgrimage and central figure of the Bhakti movement led by saints such as Tukaram and Dnyaneshwar, cast using the traditional lost-wax method.

name: Devanagari Stone Inscription
significance: This inscribed slab records a land grant in early Devanagari script under the Shilahara dynasty, offering historians valuable evidence of local administration and land revenue practice in 12th-century Maharashtra.

name: Shivaji Maharaj's Royal Letter (Patra)
significance: A modi-script administrative letter (patra) bearing the royal seal, instructing a local Deshmukh on revenue collection. Modi script documents like this remain a primary source for reconstructing Maratha administrative history.

name: Peshwa-era Sanskrit Manuscript
significance: A hand-copied Sanskrit treatise on astrology with marginal Marathi annotations, illustrating the bilingual scholarly culture maintained in Peshwa-period Pune households and pathshalas.

name: Hand-painted Paithani Saree
significance: Woven in Paithan using the traditional tapestry technique, this saree's peacock-motif pallu (Mor Pankh) exemplifies a weaving tradition patronised by the Peshwas that survives among Maharashtra's artisan families today.

name: Traditional Nauvari Drape Ensemble
significance: The nine-yard nauvari sari, draped in the kasta style, was favoured for its practicality during fieldwork and is closely associated with the image of working and warrior women of the era.

name: Kolhapuri Pheta (Turban)
significance: The Kolhapuri pheta's distinctive forward tilt was historically worn by Maratha nobility and remains a symbol of regional pride, still tied for ceremonial occasions across western Maharashtra.

name: Maratha Gilded Wall Hanging
significance: Reflecting the painting workshops patronised by Maratha courts of the Deccan, this gilded panel depicts a court scene and demonstrates the ornamental gesso-and-foil technique used in formal commissions of the period.
